topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud. I clicked it open, my heart giving a sudden, heavy lurch against my ribs. The subject line read, stark and unforgiving: "Update Regarding [Name]'s Celebration." My eyes scanned the text, the words swimming before my gaze. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party scheduled for Saturday has been cancelled." A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outwards like spilled ink. I reread the sentence three times, searching for a typo, a mistake that would make it all seem unreal. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling slightly. Who would be the first to know? I quickly minimized the window, the silence of my apartment suddenly deafening, and walked to the window, staring blankly at the ordinary street below, feeling a strange, hollow emptiness settle deep within my chest.

[story 2]

I was halfway through making coffee when the notification popped up. My hand froze above the mug, the hot liquid suddenly feeling irrelevant. It was a text message, short and brutally final: "Heads up: The surprise gathering is off." A sharp intake of breath escaped my lungs, and I gripped the counter so tightly my knuckles turned white. My mind raced, cycling through possibilities—a wrong number, a prank, a catastrophic scheduling error. I snatched my phone, refreshing the message thread, but nothing new appeared. The realization dawned slowly, a creeping chill washing over my skin. I stood there, swaying slightly, the usual energy draining out of me. I needed to call someone, anyone, but my throat felt thick, refusing to form a coherent sound.

[story 3]

The glossy invitation, which I had tucked away for safekeeping, seemed to mock me from the desk. I picked it up, the heavy cardstock feeling brittle in my hand. The cancellation notice, printed on slightly thicker paper, confirmed the unthinkable. My breath hitched, catching somewhere in my throat. I dropped the invitation onto the wooden surface, the sound barely audible. A wave of profound disorientation washed over me; the room seemed to tilt slightly. I began pacing the small space, my steps quick and uneven, my gaze darting around the room as if expecting the news to materialize from the shadows. Every muscle in my body felt taut, coiled tight with an unspent tension.
